subject: Physics
q: The work function of a substance is $4.0 \mathrm{eV}$. Then longest wavelength of light that can cause photoelectron emission from this substance approximately
question: The work function of a substance is $4.0 \mathrm{eV}$. Then longest wavelength of light that can cause photoelectron emission from this substance approximately
solution:
$\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\lambda}=\mathrm{W}$
$\lambda_{\text {longest }}=\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\mathrm{W}}=\frac{6.6 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^8}{4.0 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}}$
$\Rightarrow \lambda_{\text {longest }} \approx 310 \mathrm{~nm}$.

If one in <math><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></math> photons ejects an election, total number of electrons ejected in <math><mn>1</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"></mi><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> is <math><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow></msup></math> . <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>h</mi><mi>c</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1240</mn><mi></mi><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>,</mo><mi></mi><mn>1</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1.6</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>19</mn></mrow></msup><mi>J</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>,</mo><mi></mi></math> then <math><mi>x</mi></math> is ___________
solution:
Energy of photon. E=1240310 nm=4eV>2eV (So photoelectric effect will take place)
=4×1.6×10-19=6.4×10-19joule
Number of photons falling per second
=6.4×10-5×16.4×10-19=1014
Number of photoelectron emitted per second
=1014103=1011

subject: Physics
q: The stopping potential in the context of photoelectric effect depends on the following property of incident electromagnetic radiation:
question: The stopping potential in the context of photoelectric effect depends on the following property of incident electromagnetic radiation:
solution:
According to Einstein’s photoelectric equation, stopping potential depends on frequency as

hν-hν∘=eVV=hν-hν∘e

Hence stopping potential depends on frequency.

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements: one is labelled as Assertion A and the other is labelled as Reason R.

Assertion A : The photoelectric effect does not take place, if the energy of the incident radiation is less than the work function of a metal.

Reason R : Kinetic energy of the photoelectrons is zero, if the energy of the incident radiation is equal to the work function of a metal.
question:
Given below are two statements: one is labelled as Assertion A and the other is labelled as Reason R.

Assertion A : The photoelectric effect does not take place, if the energy of the incident radiation is less than the work function of a metal.

Reason R : Kinetic energy of the photoelectrons is zero, if the energy of the incident radiation is equal to the work function of a metal.
options: Both A and R are correct and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are correct but R is not the correct explanation of A
A is correct but R is not correct
A is not correct but R is correct
solution:
Work function of a metal is defined as the minimum amount of energy needed for ejection of photoelectron. Therefore, Assertion is correct.

Incident energy may or may not be completely absorbed by the photoelectron. Therefore, kinetic energy of a photoelectron may be zero even if the incident radiation has energy greater than work function. Hence, though the reason is correct, it is not the correct explanation of Assertion.

subject: Physics
q: The de Broglie wavelength of an electron having kinetic energy <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>E</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>λ</mi></math>. If the kinetic energy of electron becomes <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>E</mi><mn>4</mn></mfrac></math>, then its de-Broglie wavelength will be:
question: The de Broglie wavelength of an electron having kinetic energy <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>E</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>λ</mi></math>. If the kinetic energy of electron becomes <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>E</mi><mn>4</mn></mfrac></math>, then its de-Broglie wavelength will be:
solution:
The de-Broglie wavelength in the first case is 

λ=h2mE

In the second case the kinetic energy is K=E4.

Let the de-Broglie wavelength be λ'.

The value of wavelength is

λ'=h2mE4=2h2mE=2λ

subject: Physics
q: The de-Broglie wavelength <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><msub><mrow><mi>λ</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>B</mi></mrow></msub></mrow></mfenced></math> associated with the electron orbiting in the second excited state of hydrogen atom is related to that in the ground state <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><msub><mrow><mi>λ</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>G</mi></mrow></msub></mrow></mfenced></math> by:
question: The de-Broglie wavelength <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><msub><mrow><mi>λ</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>B</mi></mrow></msub></mrow></mfenced></math> associated with the electron orbiting in the second excited state of hydrogen atom is related to that in the ground state <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><msub><mrow><mi>λ</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>G</mi></mrow></msub></mrow></mfenced></math> by:
solution:
We know that wavelength is inversely proportional to momentum, let λB is de-Broglie Wavelength corresponds to momentum PB, similarly  let λG is Wavelength at ground state corresponds to momentumPG, 
λBλG= PGPB= mvGmvB, and velocity, V∝zn, where Z is the atomic number, and n is a number of orbits, here, nG=1 (ground state), nB=3 (second excited state). So, λBλG= nBnG= 31⇒λB=3λG

subject: Physics
q: A proton, a neutron, an electron and an <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math>-particle have same energy. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>λ</mi><mi>p</mi></msub><mo>,</mo><msub><mi>λ</mi><mi>n</mi></msub><mo>,</mo><msub><mi>λ</mi><mi>e</mi></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>λ</mi><mi>α</mi></msub></math> are the de Broglie's wavelengths of proton, neutron, electron and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math> particle respectively, then choose the correct relation from the following
question: A proton, a neutron, an electron and an <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math>-particle have same energy. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>λ</mi><mi>p</mi></msub><mo>,</mo><msub><mi>λ</mi><mi>n</mi></msub><mo>,</mo><msub><mi>λ</mi><mi>e</mi></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>λ</mi><mi>α</mi></msub></math> are the de Broglie's wavelengths of proton, neutron, electron and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math> particle respectively, then choose the correct relation from the following
solution:
The de-Broglie wavelength λ = h2m(K.E.)

where, h and K.E. are constant

λ∝1m

m= Mass of the particle

Thus, with increasing mass of particle de-Broglie wavelength decreases.

Order of mass me<mp<mn<mα

Order of de-Broglie wavelength is λa<λn<λp<λe

subject: Physics
q: In an experiment with photoelectric effect, the stopping potential,
question: In an experiment with photoelectric effect, the stopping potential,
options: increases with increase in the intensity of the incident light
decreases with increase in the intensity of the incident light
increases with increase in the wavelength of the incident light
is $\left(\frac{1}{\mathrm{e}}\right)$ times the maximum kinetic energy of the emitted photoelectrons
solution:
From Einstein photoelectric equation
$\frac{h c}{\lambda}=\phi+e V_S$

Maximum K.E. $=(\mathrm{K})_{\max }=e V s$
So, $V_S=\frac{(\mathrm{K})_{\max }}{e}$

subject: Physics
q: The work functions of Aluminium and Gold are 4.1 eV and 5.1 eV respectively. The ratio of the slope of the stopping potential versus frequency plot for Gold to that of Aluminium is
question: The work functions of Aluminium and Gold are 4.1 eV and 5.1 eV respectively. The ratio of the slope of the stopping potential versus frequency plot for Gold to that of Aluminium is
solution:
The data given is 

ϕAl=4.1 eV

ϕAu=5.1 eV

The energy is given by 

hν=ϕ+KE⇒hν=ϕ+eV0

where V0 is the stopping potential and K=eV0

Thus, the above equation can be written as

eV0=hν-ϕ

⇒V0=hνe-ϕe

The equation is of the form of y=mx+c. The slope is 

m=he.

Since the Planck constant h and charge of an electron is a constant e, the slope does not depend on the work function. Hence the ratio of the slope of the plot of stopping potential and frequency for both the given metals is 1.

question:
Match list-I (Fundamental Experiment) with List-II (its conclusion) and select the correct option from the choices given below the list:
 
   List-I    List-II
A Franck-Hertz Experiment (i) Particle nature of light
B Photo-electric experiment (ii) Discrete energy levels of the atom
C Davison-Germer Experiment (iii) Wave nature of electron
    (iv) Structure of atom
options: <math><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo>-</mo><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">v</mi></mrow></mfenced><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mo>;</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><mo>-</mo><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>;</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mo>-</mo><mo>(</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>)</mo></math>

<math><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo>-</mo><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi></mrow></mfenced><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mo>;</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><mo>-</mo><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">v</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>;</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mo>-</mo><mo>(</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>)</mo></math>

<math><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo>-</mo><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi></mrow></mfenced><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mo>;</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><mo>-</mo><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">v</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>;</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mo>-</mo><mo>(</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>)</mo></math>
solution: Frank-Hertz's experiment demonstrated the existence of excited states in mercury atoms helping to confirm the quantum theory which predicted that electrons occupied only discrete quantized energy states. The photo-electric experiment demonstrated that photon is the particle of light, which can transfer momentum and energy due to collision. Davidson-Germer's experiment shows the wave nature of electrons.

subject: Physics
q: An electron of mass <math><mi>m</mi></math> and magnitude of charge <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>e</mi></math> at rest, gets accelerated by a constant electric field <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>E</mi></math>. The rate of change of de-Broglie wavelength of this electron at a time <math><mi>t</mi></math> is (ignore relativistic effects)
question: An electron of mass <math><mi>m</mi></math> and magnitude of charge <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>e</mi></math> at rest, gets accelerated by a constant electric field <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>E</mi></math>. The rate of change of de-Broglie wavelength of this electron at a time <math><mi>t</mi></math> is (ignore relativistic effects)
solution:
The acceleration of the electron is

a=eEm

The velocity of the electron as a function of time is

v=at=eEmt

The de-Broglie wavelength is

λ=hmv

λ=hmeEmt

λ=heEt
dλdt=-heEt2

subject: Physics
q: The anode voltage of a photocell is kept fixed. The wavelength <math> <mrow> <mi>λ</mi> </mrow> </math> of the light falling on the cathode is gradually changed. The plate current <math> <mrow> <mtext>I</mtext> </mrow> </math> of the photocell varies as follows :
question: The anode voltage of a photocell is kept fixed. The wavelength <math> <mrow> <mi>λ</mi> </mrow> </math> of the light falling on the cathode is gradually changed. The plate current <math> <mrow> <mtext>I</mtext> </mrow> </math> of the photocell varies as follows :
solution: As λ is increased, there will be a value of λ above which photo-electron will be cease to come out, so photo-current will becomes zero.

subject: Physics
q: When radiation of wavelength <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> is used to illuminate a metallic surface, the stopping potential is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>. When the same surface is illuminated with radiation of wavelength <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math>, the stopping potential is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><mn>4</mn></mfrac></math>. If the threshold wavelength for the metallic surface is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>nλ</mi></math> then value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi></math> will be :
question: When radiation of wavelength <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> is used to illuminate a metallic surface, the stopping potential is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>. When the same surface is illuminated with radiation of wavelength <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math>, the stopping potential is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><mn>4</mn></mfrac></math>. If the threshold wavelength for the metallic surface is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>nλ</mi></math> then value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi></math> will be :
solution:
hcλ=ϕ+eV ...........(1)

hc3λ=ϕ+eV4      ---------(2)

from (1) & (2)

hcλ1-13=34ev

hcλ23=34eV

eV=89hcλ

hcλ=ϕ+89hcλ

ϕ=hc9λ=hcλth

λth=9λ

∴k=9

subject: Physics
q: The kinetic energy of an electron, α-particle and a proton are given as 4K, 2K and K respectively. The de-Broglie wavelength associated with electron (λe), α-particle (λα) and the proton (λp) are as follows:
question: The kinetic energy of an electron, α-particle and a proton are given as 4K, 2K and K respectively. The de-Broglie wavelength associated with electron (λe), α-particle (λα) and the proton (λp) are as follows:
solution:
The formula to calculate the de Broglie wavelength of a particle is given by

λ=h2mK   ...1

Now, the masses of the given particles are interrelated by the following relations:

me≈mp1837mα=4mp

The de Broglie wavelength for electron is given by

λe=h2me4K= h81837mpK   ...2

The de Broglie wavelength for proton is given by

λp=h2mp2K= h4mpK   ...3

And, the de Broglie wavelength of alpha particle is given by

λα=h2mαK= h24mpK= h8mpK   ...4

From equation (2), (3) and (4), it can be concluded that λe>λp>λα.

subject: Physics
q: Two electrons are moving with non-relativistic speeds perpendicular to each other. If corresponding de brogile wavelengths are <math><msub><mrow><mi>λ</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></math> and <math><msub><mrow><mi>λ</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></math>, their de brogile wavelength in the frame of reference attached to their centre of mass is:
question: Two electrons are moving with non-relativistic speeds perpendicular to each other. If corresponding de brogile wavelengths are <math><msub><mrow><mi>λ</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></math> and <math><msub><mrow><mi>λ</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></math>, their de brogile wavelength in the frame of reference attached to their centre of mass is:
solution:
Momentum of each electron hλ1 i^ and hλ2j^

Velocity of centre of mass

Vcm=h2mλ1 i^+h2mλ2j^

Velocity of 1st particle about centre of mass

Vcm=h2mλ1 i^-h2mλ2j^

λcm=hh24λ12+h24λ22=2λ1 λ2λ12+λ22

subject: Physics
q: If \(\lambda\) and \(K\) are de Broglie wavelength and kinetic energy, respectively, of a particle with constant mass. The correct graphical representation for the particle will be
question: If \(\lambda\) and \(K\) are de Broglie wavelength and kinetic energy, respectively, of a particle with constant mass. The correct graphical representation for the particle will be
solution:
$\begin{aligned}
& \lambda=\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\mathrm{mv}}=\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\sqrt{2 \mathrm{mK}}} \\
& \lambda^2=\frac{\mathrm{h}^2}{2 \mathrm{~m}}\left(\frac{1}{\mathrm{k}}\right) \\
& \mathrm{Y}=\mathrm{cx}^2
\end{aligned}$

Upward facing parabola passing through origin.

subject: Physics
q: The de-Broglie wavelength of a particle having kinetic energy <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>E</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>λ</mi><mo>.</mo></math> How much extra energy must be given to this particle so that the de-Broglie wavelength reduces to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>75</mn><mo>%</mo></math> of the initial value?
question: The de-Broglie wavelength of a particle having kinetic energy <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>E</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>λ</mi><mo>.</mo></math> How much extra energy must be given to this particle so that the de-Broglie wavelength reduces to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>75</mn><mo>%</mo></math> of the initial value?
solution:
Recall the formula of de-Broglie wavelength, λ=hp, here p is the linear momentum of the particle, now use the relation between linear momentum and kinetic energy, p=2Em⇒E∝1λ2⇒E=kλ2

and E'=16k9λ2⇒ΔE=7E9.

subject: Physics
q:
Paragraph: Wave property of electrons implies that they will show diffraction effects. Davisson and Germer demonstrated this by diffracting electrons from crystals. The law governing the diffraction from a crystal is obtained by requiring that electron waves reflected from the planes of atoms in a crystal interfere constructively (see in figure).
Question: Electrons accelerated by potential $V$ are diffracted from a crystal. If $d=1 Å$ and $i=30^{\circ}, V$ should be about $\left(\mathrm{h}=6.6 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{Js}, \mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{e}}=9.1 \times 10^{-31} \mathrm{~kg}, \mathrm{e}=1.6 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{C}\right)$
question:
Paragraph: Wave property of electrons implies that they will show diffraction effects. Davisson and Germer demonstrated this by diffracting electrons from crystals. The law governing the diffraction from a crystal is obtained by requiring that electron waves reflected from the planes of atoms in a crystal interfere constructively (see in figure).

Match List‐I (Experiment performed) with List‐II (Phenomena discovered/associated)and select the correct option from the options given below the lists

(a) Davisson and Germer experiment i Wave nature of electrons 
b Millikan's oil drop experiment ii Charge of an electron 
c Rutherford experiment  iii Quantisation of energy levels
d Franck-Hertz experiment (iv) Existence of the nucleus
question:
Match List‐I (Experiment performed) with List‐II (Phenomena discovered/associated)and select the correct option from the options given below the lists

  List-I   List-II
(a) Davisson and Germer experiment i Wave nature of electrons 
b Millikan's oil drop experiment ii Charge of an electron 
c Rutherford experiment  iii Quantisation of energy levels
d Franck-Hertz experiment (iv) Existence of the nucleus
solution:
The oil drop experiment was to measure the specific charge of an electron.

Davisson & Germer experiment proved the wave nature of electrons.

Rutherford experiment proved the presence of a nucleus, while the Franck-Hertz experiment established the Quantisation of energy levels.

subject: Physics
q:
A metal exposed to light of wavelength 800 nm and emits photoelectrons with a certain kinetic energy. The maximum kinetic energy of photo-electron doubles when light of wavelength 500 nm is used. The work function of the metal is

(Take hc=1230 eV-nm).
question:
A metal exposed to light of wavelength 800 nm and emits photoelectrons with a certain kinetic energy. The maximum kinetic energy of photo-electron doubles when light of wavelength 500 nm is used. The work function of the metal is

(Take hc=1230 eV-nm).
solution:
According to Einstein's photoelectric equation

kmax=hcλ-ϕ

⇒k1=1230800-ϕ       ...1

And ⇒k2=1230500-ϕ

Since, k2=2k1

⇒2k1=1230500-ϕ    ...2

Eliminating k1 from (1) and (2) we get

0=1230500-1230400+ϕ

⇒ϕ=0.615 eV

subject: Physics
q: A beam of light has two wavelengths of $4972 Å$ and $6216 Å$ with a total intensity of $3.6 \times 10^{-3}$ $\mathrm{Wm}^{-2}$ equally distributed among the two wavelengths. The beam falls normally on an area of $1 \mathrm{~cm}^2$ of a clean metallic surface of work function $2.3 \mathrm{eV}$. Assume that there is no loss of light by reflection and that each capable photon ejects one electron. The number of photoelectrons liberated in $2 \mathrm{~s}$ is approximately:
question: A beam of light has two wavelengths of $4972 Å$ and $6216 Å$ with a total intensity of $3.6 \times 10^{-3}$ $\mathrm{Wm}^{-2}$ equally distributed among the two wavelengths. The beam falls normally on an area of $1 \mathrm{~cm}^2$ of a clean metallic surface of work function $2.3 \mathrm{eV}$. Assume that there is no loss of light by reflection and that each capable photon ejects one electron. The number of photoelectrons liberated in $2 \mathrm{~s}$ is approximately:
solution:
Given, $\lambda_1=4972 Å$
and $\lambda_2=6216 Å$
and $\mathrm{I}=3.6 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{Wm}^{-2}$
Intensity associated with each wavelength
$$
=\frac{3.6 \times 10^{-3}}{2}=1.8 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{Wm}^{-2}
$$
Work function $\phi=\mathrm{hv}=\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\lambda}$
$$
\begin{aligned}
&=\frac{\left(6.62 \times 10^{-34}\right)\left(3 \times 10^8\right)}{\lambda} \\
&=\frac{12.4 \times 10^3}{\lambda} \mathrm{ev}
\end{aligned}
$$


for different wavelengths
$$
\begin{array}{r}
\phi_1=\frac{12.4 \times 10^3}{\lambda_1}=\frac{12.4 \times 10^3}{4972}=2.493 \mathrm{eV} \\
=3.984 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{~J} \\
\phi_2=\frac{12.4 \times 10^3}{\lambda_2}=\frac{12.4 \times 10^3}{6216}=1.994 \mathrm{eV} \\
=3.184 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{~J}
\end{array}
$$
Work function for metallic surface $\phi=2.3$ $\mathrm{eV}$ (given)
$\phi_2 < \phi$
Therefore, $\phi_2$ will not contribute in this process.
Now, no. of electrons per $\mathrm{m}^2-\mathrm{s}=$ no. of photons per $\mathrm{m}^2-\mathrm{s}$
no. of electrons per $\mathrm{m}^2-\mathrm{s}$
$$
\begin{aligned}
&=\frac{1.8 \times 10^{-3}}{3.984 \times 10^{-19}} \times 10^{-4} \\
&\left(\because 1 \mathrm{~cm}^2=10^{-4} \mathrm{~m}^2\right)=0.45 \times 10^{12}
\end{aligned}
$$
So, the number of photo electrons liberated
in 2 sec.
$=0.45 \times 10^{12} \times 2$
$=9 \times 10^{11}$

subject: Physics
q: Proton <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">P</mi><mo>)</mo></math> and electron <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi><mo>)</mo></math> will have same de-Broglie wavelength when the ratio of their momentum is (assume, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>m</mi><mi>p</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>1849</mn></math> <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>m</mi><mi>e</mi></msub></math>)
question: Proton <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">P</mi><mo>)</mo></math> and electron <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi><mo>)</mo></math> will have same de-Broglie wavelength when the ratio of their momentum is (assume, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>m</mi><mi>p</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>1849</mn></math> <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>m</mi><mi>e</mi></msub></math>)
solution:
The De Broglie wavelength is λ=hmv

The momentum is given by p=mv

The momentum of proton is pp=mpvp

The momentum of electron is  p'=meve

Since h is the Planck's constant and it is given that both wavelengths are same, i.e.

λp=λe, so pp=p'.

subject: Physics
q: In a photoelectric effect experiment a light of frequency <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn></math> times the threshold frequency is made to fall on the surface of photosensitive material. Now if the frequency is halved and intensity is doubled, the number of photo electrons emitted will be:
question: In a photoelectric effect experiment a light of frequency <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn></math> times the threshold frequency is made to fall on the surface of photosensitive material. Now if the frequency is halved and intensity is doubled, the number of photo electrons emitted will be:
solution:
Given, fi=f0. Now, f=fi2=f02.

Since f02<f0 i.e. the incident frequency is less than threshold frequency. Hence, there will be no emission of photoelectrons.

Therefore, current =0 and no photo electrons will be emitted.

subject: Physics
q:
Light source having wavelength $331$ nm is used to generate photo-electrons whose stopping potential is $0.2$ V. The work function of the used metal in the experiment is $\alpha \times 10^{-19}$ J. The value of $\alpha$ is _____.
($h = 6.62 \times 10^{-34}$ J s, $e = 1.6 \times 10^{-19}$ C and $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s)
question:
Light source having wavelength $331$ nm is used to generate photo-electrons whose stopping potential is $0.2$ V. The work function of the used metal in the experiment is $\alpha \times 10^{-19}$ J. The value of $\alpha$ is _____.
($h = 6.62 \times 10^{-34}$ J s, $e = 1.6 \times 10^{-19}$ C and $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s)
solution:
Using Einstein's photoelectric equation:

$\dfrac{hc}{\lambda} = \Phi + eV_0$

The energy of the incident photon is:

$E = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda} = \dfrac{6.62 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^8}{331 \times 10^{-9}}$

$E = \dfrac{19.86 \times 10^{-26}}{331 \times 10^{-9}} = 6 \times 10^{-19}$ J

The maximum kinetic energy of the photoelectrons is:

$K_{\text{max}} = eV_0 = 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 0.2 = 0.32 \times 10^{-19}$ J

The work function $\Phi$ is:

$\Phi = E - K_{\text{max}}$

$\Phi = 6 \times 10^{-19} - 0.32 \times 10^{-19} = 5.68 \times 10^{-19}$ J

Comparing with $\alpha \times 10^{-19}$ J, we get $\alpha = 5.68$.

Answer: $5.68$

subject: Physics
q: An electron having de-Broglie wavelength <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>λ</mi></math> is incident on a target in a <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi></math>-ray tube. Cut-off wavelength of emitted <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi></math>-ray is:
question: An electron having de-Broglie wavelength <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>λ</mi></math> is incident on a target in a <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi></math>-ray tube. Cut-off wavelength of emitted <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi></math>-ray is:
solution:
λ=hmv

Kinetic energy, P22 m=h22 mλ2=hcλc

λc=2 mλ2ch

subject: Physics
q: A monochromatic neon lamp with wavelength of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>670</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>nm</mi></math> illuminates a photo-sensitive material which has a stopping voltage of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>48</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>. What will be the stopping voltage if the source light is changed with another source of wavelength of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>474</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi>nm</mi></math> ?
question: A monochromatic neon lamp with wavelength of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>670</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>nm</mi></math> illuminates a photo-sensitive material which has a stopping voltage of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>48</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>. What will be the stopping voltage if the source light is changed with another source of wavelength of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>474</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi>nm</mi></math> ?
solution:
eVs=hcλ-ϕ

eV'-V=hc1λ'-1λ

V'=hce1λ'-1λ+V

=12401474-1670+0.48=1.25eV

subject: Physics
q: Two streams of photons, possessing energies to five and ten times the work function of metal are incident on the metal surface successively. The ratio of the maximum velocities of the photoelectron emitted, in the two cases respectively, will be
question: Two streams of photons, possessing energies to five and ten times the work function of metal are incident on the metal surface successively. The ratio of the maximum velocities of the photoelectron emitted, in the two cases respectively, will be
solution:
Using Einstein's photoelectric equation,

Maximum kinetic energy, KEmax=E-ϕ, where, E is energy of photon and ϕ is work function.

For two cases, 

12mv12=5ϕ-ϕ=4ϕ and 12mv22=10ϕ-ϕ=9ϕ

Thus, the ratio v1v2=49=23=2:3

subject: Physics
q: A laser light of wavelength <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>660</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi>nm</mi></math> is used to weld Retina detachment. If a laser pulse of width <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi>ms</mi></math> and power <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">0</mi><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kW</mi></math> is used, the approximate number of photons in the pulse are (Take Planck's Constant, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>h</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>6.62</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>−</mo><mn>34</mn></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mo>)</mo></math>
question: A laser light of wavelength <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>660</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi>nm</mi></math> is used to weld Retina detachment. If a laser pulse of width <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi>ms</mi></math> and power <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">0</mi><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kW</mi></math> is used, the approximate number of photons in the pulse are (Take Planck's Constant, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>h</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>6.62</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>−</mo><mn>34</mn></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mo>)</mo></math>
solution:
The energy of photon is given by E=hcλ.

PowerP is given as, P=Et=nhcλt

From the above equation, the number of photons is n=Pλthc

=0.5×103×660×10-9×60×10-36.6×10-34×3×108

Hence, number of photons in the pulse are=100×1018=1020.

subject: Physics
q: For the photoelectric effect, the maximum kinetic energy Ek of the photoelectrons is plotted against the frequency (v) of the incident photons as shown in figure. The slope of the graph gives
question: For the photoelectric effect, the maximum kinetic energy Ek of the photoelectrons is plotted against the frequency (v) of the incident photons as shown in figure. The slope of the graph gives
options: Ratio of Planck’s constant to electric charge
solution: Comparing Einstein's equation Kmax=hv-hv0, with y=mx+c, we get slope, m=h, which is Planck’s constant.

subject: Physics
q: The ratio of de-Broglie wavelength of an <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math>-particle and a proton accelerated from rest by the same potential is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>1</mn><msqrt><mi>m</mi></msqrt></mfrac></math> , the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> is:
question: The ratio of de-Broglie wavelength of an <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math>-particle and a proton accelerated from rest by the same potential is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>1</mn><msqrt><mi>m</mi></msqrt></mfrac></math> , the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> is:
solution:
As we know, de Broglie wavelength in terms of energy of particle is given by, λ=12mE.

Energy gained by proton, E=qV.

Therefore,

λαλp=h2mαqαVh2mpqpV

⇒λαλp=18

⇒m=8

subject: Physics
q: A particle <math><mi>A</mi></math> of mass <math><mi>m</mi></math> and initial velocity <math><mi>v</mi></math> collides with a particle <math><mi>B</mi></math> of mass <math><mfrac><mrow><mi>m</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfrac></math> which is at rest. The collision is head on, and elastic. The ratio of the de-Broglie wavelengths <math><msub><mrow><mi>λ</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>A</mi></mrow></msub></math> to <math><msub><mrow><mi>λ</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>B</mi></mrow></msub></math> after the collision is:
question: A particle <math><mi>A</mi></math> of mass <math><mi>m</mi></math> and initial velocity <math><mi>v</mi></math> collides with a particle <math><mi>B</mi></math> of mass <math><mfrac><mrow><mi>m</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfrac></math> which is at rest. The collision is head on, and elastic. The ratio of the de-Broglie wavelengths <math><msub><mrow><mi>λ</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>A</mi></mrow></msub></math> to <math><msub><mrow><mi>λ</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>B</mi></mrow></msub></math> after the collision is:
solution:
mV=m V A + m 2 V B

V= V A + V B 2    −−−−−−−−−−−−  (i)

V= V B − V A     −−−−−−−−−−−−  (ii)

Solving (i) and (ii)

2V= 3 V B 2

∴ V B = 4V 3

V A = V B −V= V 3

P A = mV 3

P B = m 2 . 4V 3

λ A λ B = h× P B P A ×h = 2mV 3 mV 3 =2

subject: Physics
q: A particle moving with kinetic energy <math><mi>E</mi></math> has de Broglie wavelength <math><mi>λ</mi></math> . If energy <math><mi mathvariant="normal">Δ</mi><mi>E</mi></math> is added to its energy, the wavelength become <math><mfrac><mrow><mi>λ</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfrac></math> . Value of <math><mi mathvariant="normal">Δ</mi><mi>E</mi><mo>,</mo></math> is:
question: A particle moving with kinetic energy <math><mi>E</mi></math> has de Broglie wavelength <math><mi>λ</mi></math> . If energy <math><mi mathvariant="normal">Δ</mi><mi>E</mi></math> is added to its energy, the wavelength become <math><mfrac><mrow><mi>λ</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfrac></math> . Value of <math><mi mathvariant="normal">Δ</mi><mi>E</mi><mo>,</mo></math> is:
solution:
λ=h2KEm⇒λ∝1KE
λλ2=KEfKEi
4KEi=KEf
⇒ΔE=4KEi-KEi=3KE=3E

subject: Physics
q: A certain metallic surface is illuminated by monochromatic radiation of wavelength $\lambda$. The stopping potential for photoelectric current for this radiation is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><msub><mrow><mo mathvariant="italic"> </mo><mi mathvariant="italic">V</mi></mrow><mn>0</mn></msub><mo>.</mo></math> If the same surface is illuminated with a radiation of wavelength <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi>λ</mi><mo>,</mo></math> the stopping potential is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>V</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mo>.</mo></math> The threshold wavelength of this surface for photoelectric effect is___________<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>λ</mi><mo>.</mo></math>
question: A certain metallic surface is illuminated by monochromatic radiation of wavelength $\lambda$. The stopping potential for photoelectric current for this radiation is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><msub><mrow><mo mathvariant="italic"> </mo><mi mathvariant="italic">V</mi></mrow><mn>0</mn></msub><mo>.</mo></math> If the same surface is illuminated with a radiation of wavelength <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi>λ</mi><mo>,</mo></math> the stopping potential is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>V</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mo>.</mo></math> The threshold wavelength of this surface for photoelectric effect is___________<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>λ</mi><mo>.</mo></math>
solution:
KE=hcλ-ϕhc

e3V0=hcλ0-ϕ  …i

eV0=hc2λ0-ϕ  …ii

Using i and ii,

ϕ=hc4λ0=hcλt

λt=4λ0

subject: Physics
q: The de Broglie wavelength of an oxygen molecule at $27^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is $x \times 10^{-12} \mathrm{~m}$. The value of $x$ is (take Planck's constant $=6.63 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{~J}. \mathrm{s}$, Boltzmann constant $=1.38 \times 10^{-23} \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{K}$, mass of oxygen molecule $=5.31 \times 10^{-26} \mathrm{~kg}$)
question: The de Broglie wavelength of an oxygen molecule at $27^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is $x \times 10^{-12} \mathrm{~m}$. The value of $x$ is (take Planck's constant $=6.63 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{~J}. \mathrm{s}$, Boltzmann constant $=1.38 \times 10^{-23} \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{K}$, mass of oxygen molecule $=5.31 \times 10^{-26} \mathrm{~kg}$)
solution:
The de Broglie wavelength is $\lambda = \frac{h}{mv}$. For an ideal gas, $v_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{3kT}{m}}$
Therefore: $\lambda = \frac{h}{\sqrt{3mkT}}$
Substituting values: $\lambda = \frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34}}{\sqrt{3 \times 5.31 \times 10^{-26} \times 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \times 300}}$
$\lambda = \frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34}}{\sqrt{6.62 \times 10^{-46}}} = \frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34}}{2.57 \times 10^{-23}}$
$\lambda = 2.58 \times 10^{-11}$ m $= 25.8 \times 10^{-12}$ m, so $x \approx 26$

subject: Physics
q: An electron of mass ' m ' with an initial velocity $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}=\mathrm{v}_0 \hat{i}\left(\mathrm{v}_0\gt0\right)$ enters an electric field $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}}=-\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{o}} \hat{\mathrm{k}}$. If the initial de Broglie wavelength is $\lambda_0$, the value after time t would be
question: An electron of mass ' m ' with an initial velocity $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}=\mathrm{v}_0 \hat{i}\left(\mathrm{v}_0\gt0\right)$ enters an electric field $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}}=-\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{o}} \hat{\mathrm{k}}$. If the initial de Broglie wavelength is $\lambda_0$, the value after time t would be
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}=\mathrm{v}_0 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-\frac{\mathrm{E}_0 \mathrm{e}}{\mathrm{m}} t \hat{\mathrm{k}} \\ & |\overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}|=\sqrt{\mathrm{v}_0^2+\frac{\mathrm{E}_0^2 \mathrm{e}^2 \mathrm{t}^2}{\mathrm{~m}^2}} \\ & \lambda_0=\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\mathrm{mv}_0} \\ & \lambda^{\prime}=\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\mathrm{mv}_0 \sqrt{1+\frac{\mathrm{E}_0^2 \mathrm{e}^2 \mathrm{t}^2}{\mathrm{v}_0^2 \mathrm{~m}^2}}} \\ & \lambda^{\prime}=\frac{\lambda_0}{\sqrt{1+\frac{\mathrm{E}_0^2 \mathrm{e}^2 \mathrm{t}^2}{\mathrm{v}_0^2 \mathrm{~m}^2}}}\end{aligned}$
